questions.prompt: Three and forty-two thousandths written in standard decimal form.
questions.explanation: Worked example: 'three and forty-two thousandths' means the whole number is 3, the decimal portion is 42/1,000 = 0.042 (5.NBT.A.3a). Write 3, then a decimal point, then fill places out to thousandths: tenths = 0, hundredths = 4, thousandths = 2, giving 3.042. Common student error: writing 3.42 (which is 3 and 42 hundredths, not thousandths) or 3.0042 by adding an extra zero. Strategy: count the places you need (thousandths = three places past the decimal) and write the spelled number right-justified in those places, padding with zeros on the left.

questions.prompt: 63 ÷ 100 expressed as a decimal.
questions.explanation: Worked example: 63 ÷ 100 means split 63 into 100 equal parts; each part is 0.63 (5.NBT.A.2). Dividing by 100 shifts every digit two places to the right (or moves the decimal point two places left). Starting at 63.0 and shifting twice: 6.30 → 0.63. Common student error: writing 0.063 (shifted three places, as if dividing by 1,000) or 6.3 (shifted one place, as if dividing by 10). Strategy: count zeros in the divisor — 100 has two zeros, so the decimal point hops left two times. Use a place-value chart to verify the final answer.

questions.prompt: 10^4 in standard form.
questions.explanation: Worked example: 10^4 = 10 x 10 x 10 x 10, which is 10,000 (5.NBT.A.2). The exponent 4 means use four factors of 10. Quick rule: 10^n = a 1 followed by n zeros, so 10^4 = 1 followed by 4 zeros = 10,000. Common student error: writing 40 (multiplying 10 by 4) or 10,000.0 (adding an extra decimal). Strategy: write the 1, then count exactly n zeros after it. For homework speed, memorize 10^1=10, 10^2=100, 10^3=1,000, 10^4=10,000, 10^5=100,000 — these power-of-10 conversions appear on most Grade 5 state items.

questions.prompt: 57 ÷ 10^2 written as a decimal.
questions.explanation: Worked example: 10^2 = 100, so 57 ÷ 10^2 = 57 ÷ 100 (5.NBT.A.2). Dividing by 100 shifts every digit two places to the right (or moves the decimal point two places left). Starting at 57.0 and shifting: 57.0 → 5.70 → 0.57. So 57 ÷ 10^2 = 0.57. Common student error: writing 5.7 (shifted only one place, as if dividing by 10) or 0.057 (shifted three, as if dividing by 1,000). Strategy: read 10^2 as 'two zeros' or 'two decimal hops left.' Always rewrite 57 as 57.0 first so you can see where the decimal point starts.

questions.prompt: Operation used when a problem says 'how many groups of 5 in 75.'.
questions.explanation: Worked example: 'how many groups of 5 in 75' is a division problem (5.NBT.B.6). Compute 75 ÷ 5 = 15. There are 15 groups of 5 in 75. Common student error: multiplying (75 x 5 = 375) by confusing 'groups of' with 'times.' Strategy: 'how many groups of X' → divide by X. 'X groups of Y' → multiply X x Y. The word 'groups of' signals division when you're asking how many fit; multiplication when you're combining. Use a quick model: 5 + 5 + 5 + ... = 75 requires fifteen fives.

questions.prompt: The sum 1 1/2 + 2 3/4 as a mixed number in simplest form.
questions.explanation: Worked example: 1 1/2 + 2 3/4 (5.NF.A.1). Add wholes: 1 + 2 = 3. Convert fractions to common denom (LCD = 4): 1/2 = 2/4. Add fractions: 2/4 + 3/4 = 5/4 = 1 1/4. Total: 3 + 1 1/4 = 4 1/4. Common student error: writing 3 5/6 (adding numerators and denominators), or leaving the improper sum 13/4 without converting to mixed form. Strategy: add whole parts and fraction parts separately; convert improper fractions to mixed numbers and carry the whole part into the integer sum.

questions.prompt: The improper fraction equivalent to the mixed number 2 3/5.
questions.explanation: Worked example: 2 3/5 as an improper fraction (5.NF.B.3). Multiply whole by denominator: 2 x 5 = 10. Add numerator: 10 + 3 = 13. Keep denominator: 13/5. Check: 13 ÷ 5 = 2 remainder 3 = 2 3/5 ✓. Common student error: writing 23/5 (concatenating) or 6/5 (multiplying numerator by whole). Strategy: use the formula IMPROPER = (whole x denominator + numerator) over denominator. Always verify by converting back: divide the new numerator by the denominator and check that you get the original mixed number.

questions.prompt: The product 2 1/2 x 1 1/3 in simplest form.
questions.explanation: Worked example: 2 1/2 x 1 1/3 (5.NF.B.6). Convert to improper: 2 1/2 = 5/2; 1 1/3 = 4/3. Multiply: 5/2 x 4/3 = 20/6. Simplify: 20/6 = 10/3 = 3 1/3. Common student error: multiplying whole parts and fraction parts separately to get 2 + 1/6 = 2 1/6 (loses cross-terms). Strategy: ALWAYS convert mixed numbers to improper fractions before multiplying. Multiply tops and bottoms, then convert back to mixed form for the final answer.

questions.prompt: Volume of a rectangular prism with length 5 cm, width 3 cm, and height 4 cm.
questions.explanation: Worked example: rectangular prism volume V = length x width x height (5.MD.C.5). With l=5, w=3, h=4 cm: V = 5 x 3 x 4 = 60 cubic centimeters (cm³). Common student error: adding instead of multiplying (5+3+4=12) or forgetting CUBIC units (writing just 60 cm). Strategy: always multiply all three dimensions and use CUBIC units (cm³, in³, ft³). Visual: imagine stacking unit cubes — a 5x3 base holds 15 cubes per layer, and 4 layers = 60 cubes. NYS Grade 5 5.MD.C.5 expects unit-cube reasoning for volume.

questions.prompt: Volume of a cube with side length 4 inches.
questions.explanation: Worked example: cube volume V = side³ = s x s x s (5.MD.C.5). With s = 4 inches: V = 4 x 4 x 4 = 64 cubic inches. Common student error: writing 12 in³ (4 + 4 + 4 = 12) or 16 in³ (4 x 4 = 16, missing one factor). Strategy: a cube has three equal dimensions, so its volume is the side length CUBED (multiplied by itself three times). Always include the cubic units in your answer. NYS Grade 5 5.MD.C.5 introduces cube volume as a special case of rectangular prism.

questions.prompt: Side length of a cube with volume 27 cubic units.
questions.explanation: Worked example: cube with volume 27 cubic units — find side length (5.MD.C.5). Since V = s³, we need s where s³ = 27. Test: 3 x 3 x 3 = 27. So s = 3 units. Common student error: dividing 27 by 3 to get 9, or by 6 to get 4.5. Strategy: 'side length from volume' for a cube means take the CUBE ROOT of the volume. Memorize small cubes: 1³=1, 2³=8, 3³=27, 4³=64, 5³=125. Always include units (here 'units' since none are specified).

questions.prompt: Volume of an L-shaped figure made of two rectangular prisms with volumes 24 cm^3 and 18 cm^3.
questions.explanation: Worked example: composite figure made of two prisms with volumes 24 cm³ and 18 cm³ (5.MD.C.5c). Total volume = sum of parts: 24 + 18 = 42 cm³. Common student error: multiplying 24 x 18 (volumes don't multiply when figures are combined) or finding only one piece. Strategy: composite (or 'irregular') figures break into simpler rectangular prisms. Find each piece's volume separately using V = lwh, then ADD them. NYS Grade 5 5.MD.C.5c expects students to decompose L-shaped or T-shaped solids into rectangular prisms and add volumes.

questions.prompt: Volume of a rectangular prism with base area 12 cm^2 and height 5 cm.
questions.explanation: Worked example: prism with base area B and height h has V = B x h (5.MD.C.5b). With B = 12 cm² and h = 5 cm: V = 12 x 5 = 60 cm³. Common student error: writing 12 + 5 = 17 (adding instead) or forgetting cubic units. Strategy: the 'base area times height' formula works for ANY prism, not just rectangular ones. For a rectangular prism, the base area is just length x width — so V = (l x w) x h = l x w x h, the standard formula. This bridges Grade 5 to Grade 6 prism work.

questions.prompt: Inches in 1 foot.
questions.explanation: Worked example: 1 foot = 12 inches (5.MD.A.1). This is a memorized fact in the U.S. Customary system. Common student error: writing 10 inches (confusing with metric base 10) or 16 inches. Strategy: memorize the foundational customary equivalences — 1 foot = 12 inches, 1 yard = 3 feet, 1 mile = 5,280 feet. Build a unit-conversion chart and refer to it during practice. NYS Grade 5 5.MD.A.1 expects fluent conversion within the customary system.

questions.prompt: Feet in 1 yard.
questions.explanation: Worked example: 1 yard = 3 feet (5.MD.A.1). This is a memorized customary-system equivalence. Common student error: writing 12 feet (confusing yards with inches per foot) or 36 (multiplying feet x inches). Strategy: memorize the customary chain: 1 yard = 3 feet, 1 foot = 12 inches, so 1 yard = 36 inches. Use this for any length-conversion problem. NYS Grade 5 5.MD.A.1 directly tests these unit equivalences and asks students to convert in both directions.

questions.prompt: Inches equal to 4 feet.
questions.explanation: Worked example: 4 feet to inches (5.MD.A.1). Multiply by 12 (inches per foot): 4 x 12 = 48 inches. Common student error: dividing instead (4 ÷ 12 ≈ 0.33) or adding (4 + 12 = 16). Strategy: when converting from a LARGER unit to a SMALLER one (feet → inches), MULTIPLY. From smaller to larger, DIVIDE. Mnemonic: 'bigger unit = bigger number when shrinking,' so going from feet (4) to inches needs the bigger answer (48). Always pair the right operation with the direction of conversion.

questions.prompt: Pounds equal to 96 ounces.
questions.explanation: Worked example: 96 ounces to pounds (5.MD.A.1). Divide by 16 (ounces per pound): 96 ÷ 16 = 6 pounds. Common student error: multiplying (96 x 16) or treating 16 as if it were 10. Strategy: 1 pound = 16 ounces. Going from SMALLER unit (oz) to LARGER unit (lb) means DIVIDE. Check: 6 x 16 = 96 ✓. Memorize 1 lb = 16 oz alongside 1 ton = 2,000 lb. NYS Grade 5 5.MD.A.1 expects fluency converting customary weights and lengths in both directions.

questions.prompt: Centimeters in 1 meter.
questions.explanation: Worked example: 1 meter = 100 centimeters (5.MD.A.1). 'Centi' means hundredth, so 100 cm makes a full meter. Common student error: writing 10 cm (confusing with decimeter) or 1,000 cm (confusing with millimeter). Strategy: memorize the metric prefix tree — kilo (1,000), hecto (100), deca (10), [base unit], deci (1/10), centi (1/100), milli (1/1,000). 100 centimeters = 1 meter is a foundational metric fact. NYS Grade 5 5.MD.A.1 covers metric conversion alongside customary.

questions.prompt: Centimeters in 3.5 meters.
questions.explanation: Worked example: 3.5 meters to centimeters (5.MD.A.1). Multiply by 100 (cm per meter): 3.5 x 100 = 350 cm. Common student error: dividing (3.5 ÷ 100 = 0.035) or shifting the decimal the wrong way. Strategy: going from LARGER unit (m) to SMALLER unit (cm) means MULTIPLY. Each metric prefix step is a power of 10, so conversions just shift the decimal point. m → cm shifts the decimal TWO places right (3.5 → 350.). Check: 350 cm = 3.5 m ✓.

questions.prompt: Step needed to change 5 feet to inches.
questions.explanation: Worked example: 5 feet to inches — multiply by 12, since 1 foot = 12 inches (5.MD.A.1). 5 x 12 = 60 inches. The OPERATION you need is multiplication BY 12. Common student error: dividing by 12 (going the wrong direction) or multiplying by 10. Strategy: identify the conversion factor (inches per foot = 12), then choose the operation based on direction. LARGER unit → SMALLER unit: multiply. SMALLER → LARGER: divide. Always check by reversing the conversion.

questions.prompt: Seconds in 5 minutes.
questions.explanation: Worked example: 5 minutes to seconds (5.MD.A.1). Multiply by 60 (seconds per minute): 5 x 60 = 300 seconds. Common student error: writing 50 (confused with 1 minute = 10 seconds) or dividing. Strategy: 1 minute = 60 seconds; 1 hour = 60 minutes; 1 day = 24 hours. These are NON-decimal time conversions, so be extra careful. Going from MINUTES to SECONDS means multiply by 60. Use a unit-bridge: 5 min x 60 sec/min = 300 sec — the units cancel correctly.

questions.prompt: Total change from a $20 bill for items costing $7.85, $4.20, and $1.50.
questions.explanation: Worked example: change from $20 for items costing $7.85 + $4.20 + $1.50 (5.NBT.B.7). Total cost: 7.85 + 4.20 + 1.50 = 13.55. Change: 20.00 − 13.55 = 6.45. So $6.45. Common student error: subtracting one item at a time and losing track, or aligning decimals incorrectly. Strategy: in multi-step money problems, ADD all the costs first to get a single total, then SUBTRACT once from the amount paid. Always align decimal points when adding and subtracting dollar amounts.

questions.prompt: Minutes in 1 hour.
questions.explanation: Worked example: 1 hour = 60 minutes (5.MD.A.1). This is a memorized time-system fact. Common student error: writing 100 (confusing with decimal/metric base) or 24 (confusing hours per day). Strategy: time is NOT decimal — memorize: 1 hour = 60 minutes, 1 minute = 60 seconds, 1 day = 24 hours, 1 week = 7 days, 1 year = 365 days (366 leap). These appear constantly in NYS Grade 5 5.MD.A.1 conversion items.

questions.prompt: Ounces in 1 pound.
questions.explanation: Worked example: 1 pound = 16 ounces (5.MD.A.1). This is a memorized customary-weight fact. Common student error: writing 10 oz (confusing with decimal) or 12 oz (confusing with inches-per-foot). Strategy: memorize 1 lb = 16 oz alongside 1 ton = 2,000 lb. The customary weight chain isn't intuitive (16 instead of 10 or 12), so flashcards help. NYS Grade 5 5.MD.A.1 expects fluent customary weight conversion in both directions.

questions.prompt: Quarts in 1 gallon.
questions.explanation: Worked example: 1 gallon = 4 quarts (5.MD.A.1). This is a memorized customary-capacity fact. Common student error: writing 8 (confusing with pints per gallon) or 2 (confusing quarts per half-gallon). Strategy: customary capacity chain — 1 gallon = 4 quarts; 1 quart = 2 pints; 1 pint = 2 cups; 1 cup = 8 fluid ounces. Mnemonic 'G→Q→P→C' (Gallons-Quarts-Pints-Cups) with multipliers 4-2-2 helps. NYS Grade 5 5.MD.A.1 tests these conversions directly.

questions.prompt: Cups equal to 2 gallons.
questions.explanation: Worked example: 2 gallons to cups (5.MD.A.1). Chain conversion: 2 gal x 4 qt/gal = 8 qt; 8 qt x 2 pt/qt = 16 pt; 16 pt x 2 cups/pt = 32 cups. Common student error: only converting partway (stopping at quarts or pints) or multiplying in the wrong direction. Strategy: write the conversion chain — 1 gal = 4 qt, 1 qt = 2 pt, 1 pt = 2 cups. Multiply step by step, each time replacing one unit with the next. Total multiplier from gallons to cups: 4 x 2 x 2 = 16, so 2 gallons = 2 x 16 = 32 cups.

questions.prompt: Coordinates of point that is 0 units right and 4 units up.
questions.explanation: Worked example: 0 right and 4 up from origin = (0, 4) (5.G.A.1). Zero horizontal movement means x = 0; 4 up means y = 4. So the point is (0, 4) — on the positive y-axis. Common student error: writing (4, 0) (swapping coordinates). Strategy: ordered pair = (x, y) where x is horizontal motion and y is vertical motion. Any point ON the y-axis has x = 0; any point ON the x-axis has y = 0. NYS Grade 5 5.G.A.1 tests this distinction explicitly.

questions.prompt: Quadrant of the point (3, 4) in a 4-quadrant plane.
questions.explanation: Worked example: (3, 4) is in Quadrant I (5.G.A.1). The four-quadrant plane is divided by the x- and y-axes. Quadrant I has positive x AND positive y (top-right). Both coordinates of (3, 4) are positive, so Quadrant I. Common student error: writing Quadrant II (above the x-axis but with negative x) or 'first quadrant' with the wrong number system. Strategy: memorize quadrants — I (++), II (−+), III (−−), IV (+−). Count counterclockwise from top-right. NYS Grade 5 5.G.A.1 introduces first-quadrant points; Grade 6 expands to all four.

questions.prompt: Shape formed by connecting (1, 1), (5, 1), (5, 4), (1, 4) in order.
questions.explanation: Worked example: connect (1,1), (5,1), (5,4), (1,4) in order (5.G.B.3). Plot the points: bottom-left (1,1), bottom-right (5,1), top-right (5,4), top-left (1,4). The shape has 4 right angles and 2 pairs of equal opposite sides (length 4 and width 3) — a RECTANGLE. Common student error: calling it a square (sides aren't equal) or trapezoid (more than one pair of parallel sides). Strategy: check side lengths. Rectangle = 4 right angles + 2 pairs of equal sides. Square = 4 right angles + ALL sides equal (a special rectangle).

questions.prompt: Pattern for a graph passing through (0, 0), (1, 3), (2, 6), (3, 9).
questions.explanation: Worked example: points (0,0), (1,3), (2,6), (3,9) — find the rule (5.OA.B.3). Each y is 3 times the x: 0 = 3 x 0, 3 = 3 x 1, 6 = 3 x 2, 9 = 3 x 3. So y = 3x. Common student error: writing y = x + 3 (matches one or two points by accident but fails the rest). Strategy: test EVERY point against your proposed rule, not just one. Proportional relationships have form y = kx and pass through the origin (0, 0). The constant k is the ratio y/x.
